A full-time alpaca shearer, Dunham, of Lafayette, Ore., spends summers shearing stateside and travels to Australia in the winters. Several owners brought their alpacas to the Dreeses' for shearing, including the Calkinses, who had Dreese leave the heads of their 17 alpacas unshorn so they could trim them later. The animals were among 140 that Dunham expects to shear while in Alaska, including in the Anchorage area and on the Kenai Peninsula. The pair operate a Web site, www.atwistbeyond.com , run a booth at the state fair and place products in a few gift shops. (Photo by Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) …

title: Mount St. Elias survivors must stop making excuses
summary: Somewhere not far below the summit of Mount St. Elias, the snow is piling high on the frozen bodies of Aaron Martin and Reid Sanders. They have no need to rationalize the mistakes made on that 18,008-foot Southeast Alaska mountain in April. For John Griber and Greg Von Doersten, it is different. They are the ones left to explain how the quest for a world-record 31/2-mile ski descent went so badly wrong. They are the ones left to outline how a plan to drop 18,000 feet from summit to sea ended almost before it began with two dead and two more rescued. Both survivors were hard at explanations in the April 29 issue of Sports Illustrated. " We didn't just say Let's ski this thing,' " Von Doersten told Sports Illustrated reporters. title: Man dies after jump into lake
summary: A 25-year-old Anchorage man drowned Saturday after jumping into the chilly water of Taku Lake. Shelton Moore and friends were enjoying warm weather at the lake when Moore ran off a dock and did a cannonball, police said. Moore, described by police and friends as a weak swimmer, panicked when he hit the water, a friend said. Firefighters later measured the water at 38 degrees. Longtime friend Jerome Walker and Moore's fiancee jumped into the water to help but couldn't, said Keith Walker, Jerome's brother. Jerome Walker suffers from asthma and was overwhelmed by Moore's struggles. "He kept kicking and yelling. They just had to let him go," Keith Walker said. Moore was transported to Alaska Native Medical Center around 4 p.m. in critical condition. Emergency crews started CPR as soon as they …

title: State says no to Kenai oyster farm
summary: The state has denied an application for a floating oyster farm in the Kenai Fjords. According to the state's decision, the business would interfere with boaters, pose a safety hazard and could degrade the scenery of the surrounding national park. The proposed site is in 680-acre Paradise Cove, at the lip of Aialik Bay, where first-time oyster farmer Robert Hardy wanted to build a floating five-acre web of buoys and suspended pens. His request triggered protests from Kenai Fjords National Park, which said a commercial oyster operation doesn't mesh with the park's wilderness ethos. And recreational sailboat captains were concerned about losing one of the few safe places a small boat can set anchor in a storm. Those concerns turned out to be valid, according to the state Department of Natural…

title: Williams to drive debt down $3 billion
summary: Tulsa, Okla. -- Williams Cos. plans to issue more stock, sell more assets and make additional cuts in expenses in an effort to improve its finances by more than $3 billion over the next year. The announcement Tuesday comes less than a week after the energy trading company said it was considering several options to strengthen its balance sheet as Moody's Investors Service considers downgrading $13 billion of its debt. Since Enron Corp. filed for bankruptcy last year, the entire energy trading sector has been shaken by a loss of confidence from investors and lenders. Investors' wariness of the sector intensified with recent queries by federal energy and securities regulators into phantom trades orchestrated to boost trading volume and, in some cases, reported revenue. title: Interior wildfires unabated
summary: Fueled by black spruce and fanned by hot, windy weather, wildfires continued to devour thousands of acres across Alaska on Tuesday. Fire officials said 48 fires, including seven new ones reported Tuesday, were burning in the state. Firefighters actively fought seven fires and responded to all the new fires. The rest were only monitored. Five of the largest fires alone had burned a total of at least 246,000 acres. A fire near Chena Hot Springs had spread to 14,430 acres. Though smaller than others, the blaze posed the most potential for property damage, especially with lingering arid conditions in the Interior, officials said. "We still have severe fire weather -- low relative humidity and high winds -- and a warming trend on top of that," said Pete Buist with the Alaska Division of Forestr…

title: Tongass protection rejected
summary: No new wilderness should be added to the 17 million-acre Tongass National Forest in Southeast Alaska, the Forest Service said Thursday. In a closely watched decision that environmentalists say shows the Bush administration's lack of commitment to preserving pristine areas in national forests, the Forest Service released a draft plan that offers no new protection for the Tongass' 9.7 million roadless acres. Delighted, the timber lobby is breathing a sigh of relief. As it sees things, any new wilderness would have been a body blow for the beleaguered Southeast industry. "They made the right decision," said Owen Graham, executive director of the Ketchikan-based Alaska Forest Association. "Had they designated any new wilderness areas, it probably would have been impossible to have a viable tim…

title: Native activists to run on Green Party ticket
summary: Two Alaska Native activists have paired up as a Green Party ticket for the state's top two elective offices this fall. It will be Desa Jacobsson's second try to become governor, while Diane E. Benson's run for lieutenant governor is her first bid for public office. At a press conference Wednesday, they listed promoting education and health care and combatting violence and racism as their top priorities. title: House OKs abortion funding bill
summary: Juneau -- The House approved a bill Tuesday that could limit state funding for abortions for poor women. The measure tightens the definition of "therapeutic" abortions, which are procedures the state Medicaid program is constitutionally required to fund. The bill, which has already passed the Senate, now goes to Gov. Tony Knowles. Knowles' spokesman Bob King would not say whether the governor would veto the bill, although he noted the administration is on record opposing the measure. "We've testified this is an unwarranted intrusion of the Legislature into the doctor's examining room," King said. "They've tried this before, and it was struck down by the courts." title: Chile salmon a Costco star
summary: Salmon sales are surging at Costco, due mainly to a tight relationship between the warehouse club store and fish farmers in Chile, company buyers said Tuesday in Anchorage. Costco, based in Issaquah, Wash., buys about 600,000 pounds of fresh farmed salmon out of Chile every week, said Chris Ostrander, a corporate meat and seafood manager. In contrast, Costco buys less than 750,000 pounds of fresh Alaska wild salmon in an entire year, and that fish is sold mainly in the company's Alaska and West Coast stores. Ostrander was among speakers Tuesday at a seafood business forum sponsored by the nonprofit World Trade Center Alaska. The forum continues today at the Egan Civic & Convention Center downtown. title: Veterinary crossbow may be walrus lifesaver
summary: Anchorage -- Scientists are hoping to find a few carcasses during their trek this week to Round Island off Alaska's coast, where walruses spend the summer eating, belching and male bonding. The scientists want to find carcasses so that they can try out a veterinary crossbow on dead animals before they turn to live subjects. The veterinary crossbow could be a real lifesaver. Scientists are hoping it will be an alternative to drugging the Bristol Bay behemoths -- a tricky procedure that too often ends in death. Chad Jay and Dave Tessler, two U.S. Geological Survey scientists in Anchorage, and Joel Garlich-Miller, a scientist with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, will spend more than a week on the island where males congregate in the summer, packed in like, well, sardines. title: DEC sees more oil spills
summary: A rusted-out tank. A faulty switch. An old abandoned pipe that allows fuel to escape into a river. Spills like those have kept the state Department of Environmental Conservation hopping this year. summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Field Notes (Published: May 26, 2002) FISHING King limits rise on Susitna streams King salmon possession limits for most west Susitna River tributaries have gone up, according to the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. title: Shop for a loan just as you would shop for a home
summary: Many people put a lot of time and energy into finding the right home, but very little into obtaining the right loan. When comparing loan programs, most only look at the interest rates. So when is a low interest rate not a good business decision? To answer this, you need to understand how some closing costs and fees offset the low interest rates. Initially two closing costs affect the interest rate: the loan origination fee and discount points. The loan origination fee is what a lender charges to process and grant a loan at the current market rate. The cost is normally 1 percent of the loan amount. Discount points, paid by the seller or buyer prior to obtaining a loan, reduce the interest rate. One discount point equates to 1 percent of the loan amount. Discount points provide lenders with …
